content:
# John Chapter 4 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in John Chapter 4 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 4:1 — "the Lord" vs. "Jesus" for Greek ὁ κύριος

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "When therefore the Lord knew that the Pharisaioi heard that Yeshua makes and baptizes more disciples than Ioannes" | Preserves "the Lord"; transliterated names |
| LOGOS | "Now when the Lord knew that the Pharisees had heard that Jesus was making and baptizing more disciples than John" | Preserves "the Lord" per Sinaiticus |
| PNEUMA | "Jesus learned that the Pharisees had heard he was baptizing and making more disciples than John" | Replaces "the Lord" with "Jesus"; simplifies |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "the Lord" is the Sinaiticus reading (ὁ κύριος). PNEUMA substitutes "Jesus" which is a later manuscript variant.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's substitution noted as following a different textual tradition.

---

## 4:5 — "a town of Samaria" vs. "the Samaritan village of Sychar"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a city of the Samareia called Sychar" | Literal; "city" for πόλιν |
| LOGOS | "a town of Samaria called Sychar" | Formal; "town" |
| PNEUMA | "the Samaritan village of Sychar" | Dynamic; "village" — smaller settlement implication |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. πόλις can range from village to city; "town" is a reasonable middle ground.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:6 — "the sixth hour" vs. "about noon" for Greek ὥρα ἦν ὡς ἕκτη

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The hour was about the sixth." | Literal; ancient time reference |
| LOGOS | "It was about the sixth hour." | Formal; same |
| PNEUMA | "It was about noon." | Dynamic; converts to modern time |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "the sixth hour." Consistent with the approach to 1:39 ("the tenth hour"). PNEUMA's conversion assumes standard Jewish reckoning from sunrise.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:7 — "Give me a drink" vs. "Please give me a drink"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Give me to drink." | Literal imperative |
| LOGOS | "Give me a drink." | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "Please give me a drink." | Dynamic; adds "Please" for politeness |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. "Please" is not in the Greek. The bare imperative is characteristic of Jesus' speech in John.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:9 — "Jews have no dealings with Samaritans" vs. "Jews refused to have anything to do with Samaritans"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For Ioudaioi do not associate with Samaritans." | Literal |
| LOGOS | "(For Jews have no dealings with Samaritans.)" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "(For Jews refused to have anything to do with Samaritans.)" | Dynamic; "refused" adds active hostility |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. The Greek (οὐ συγχρῶνται) means "do not use jointly / have no dealings with." PNEUMA's "refused" intensifies the statement.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:10 — "If you knew the gift of God" — rendering differences

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "If you knew the gift of the God and who it is [that] says to you, 'Give me to drink,' you would have asked him, and he would have given to you living water." | Literal; retains articles |
| LOGOS | "If you knew the gift of God, and who it is that is saying to you, 'Give me a drink,' you would have asked him, and he would have given you living water." | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "If you only knew the gift God has for you and who you are speaking to, you would ask me, and I would give you living water." | Dynamic; adds "only"; "the gift God has for you" expands; shifts to second person |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "only" (not in Greek), changes "the gift of God" to "the gift God has for you" (interpretive expansion), and shifts the conditional structure.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:11 — "Sir" vs. "Lord" for Greek κύριε

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Lord, you have no bucket and the well is deep." | "Lord" — preserves the theological resonance |
| LOGOS | "Sir, you have nothing to draw water with, and the well is deep." | "Sir" — contextually appropriate for a stranger |
| PNEUMA | "But sir, you don't have a rope or a bucket...and this well is very deep." | "sir"; adds "rope"; "very deep" intensifies |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "Sir." At this point the woman does not yet recognize Jesus as Lord; κύριε functions as a respectful address to a stranger. PNEUMA adds "rope" and "very" not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:14 — "a spring of water welling up to eternal life" vs. "a fresh, bubbling spring"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "will become in him a spring of water springing up into eternal life" | Literal; "springing up" |
| LOGOS | "will become in him a spring of water welling up to eternal life" | Formal; "welling up" |
| PNEUMA | "becomes a fresh, bubbling spring within them, giving them eternal life" | Dynamic; "fresh, bubbling"; "giving" instead of "welling up to" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "fresh, bubbling" is vivid but adds adjectives not in the Greek. "Giving them eternal life" restructures the prepositional phrase "into eternal life" (εἰς ζωὴν αἰώνιον).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:18 — "the one you now have is not your husband" vs. "the man you're living with now isn't your husband"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [one] whom now you have is not the husband of you" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "the one you now have is not your husband" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "the man you're living with now isn't your husband" | Dynamic; "living with" interprets ἔχεις |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "living with" makes explicit the sexual cohabitation implied but not stated by ἔχεις ("have").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:21 — "Woman, believe me" vs. "Believe me, dear woman"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Believe me, woman" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "Woman, believe me" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "Believe me, dear woman" | Dynamic; adds "dear" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "dear" (as in 2:4). The formal "Woman" is respectful in the Greek register and should not be softened.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:22 — "salvation is from the Jews" vs. "salvation comes through the Jews"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the salvation is from the Ioudaioi" | Literal; with article |
| LOGOS | "salvation is from the Jews" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "salvation comes through the Jews" | Dynamic; "through" instead of "from" (ἐκ) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. The preposition ἐκ means "from/out of," indicating origin. PNEUMA's "through" suggests instrumentality — a different nuance.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:23 — "the hour is coming, and is now here" vs. "the time is coming — and it's already here"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "an hour comes, and now is" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "the hour is coming, and is now here" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "the time is coming — and it's already here" | Dynamic; "time" instead of "hour"; "already here" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. Again, PNEUMA renders ὥρα as "time" instead of "hour" — the same issue as 2:4. The Johannine "hour" is a recurring theological marker.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:26 — "I who speak to you am he" vs. "I am the one. You don't have to wait any longer." for Greek ἐγώ εἰμι, ὁ λαλῶν σοι

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I am, the [one] speaking to you." | Literal; preserves ἐγώ εἰμι as "I am" |
| LOGOS | "I who speak to you am he." | Formal; integrates the participle |
| PNEUMA | "I am the one. You don't have to wait any longer." | Dynamic; adds "You don't have to wait any longer" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds an entire sentence ("You don't have to wait any longer") that is not in the Greek text. While it captures the emotional import, it is not translation but commentary.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:27 — "They marveled" vs. "They were shocked" for Greek ἐθαύμαζον

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they were marveling that with a woman he was speaking" | Literal; "marveling" |
| LOGOS | "They marveled that he was talking with a woman" | Formal; "marveled" |
| PNEUMA | "They were shocked to find him talking to a woman, but none of them had the nerve to ask" | Dynamic; "shocked"; "had the nerve" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. θαυμάζω means "marvel/wonder" — "shocked" is stronger than the Greek warrants. "Had the nerve" is colloquial paraphrase.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:29 — "Can this be the Christ?" vs. "Could he possibly be the Messiah?"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Is this not the Christos?" | Literal; expects positive answer (μήτι) |
| LOGOS | "Can this be the Christ?" | Formal; tentative |
| PNEUMA | "Could he possibly be the Messiah?" | Dynamic; "possibly" adds tentativeness; "Messiah" instead of "Christ" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. The Greek particle μήτι expects a negative answer ("He can't be the Christ, can he?"), but the woman's question is rhetorically suggestive. PNEUMA's "possibly" adds a word, and "Messiah" is used instead of "Christ."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:35 — "the fields are white for harvest" vs. "the fields are already ripe for harvest"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "lift up the eyes of you and see the fields, that they are white for harvest" | Literal; "white" |
| LOGOS | "lift up your eyes, and see that the fields are white for harvest" | Formal; "white" |
| PNEUMA | "look around you! The fields are already ripe for harvest." | Dynamic; "ripe" instead of "white"; adds "already"; "look around you" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. The Greek λευκαί literally means "white" — describing grain fields ready for harvest. PNEUMA's "ripe" interprets the metaphor rather than translating it.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:36 — "gathering fruit for eternal life" vs. "the fruit they harvest is people brought to eternal life"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "gathers fruit into eternal life, that the [one] sowing and the [one] reaping may rejoice together" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "gathering fruit for eternal life, so that sower and reaper may rejoice together" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "the fruit they harvest is people brought to eternal life. What joy awaits both the planter and the harvester alike!" | Dynamic; interprets "fruit" as "people"; exclamatory |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "people brought to eternal life" explicitly identifies the metaphorical meaning but goes beyond translation. The exclamatory style adds emotional register not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:46 — "an official" vs. "a royal official" vs. "a government official" for Greek βασιλικός

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a certain royal [official]" | Literal; "royal" |
| LOGOS | "an official" | Formal; simplified |
| PNEUMA | "A government official" | Dynamic; modernized |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. βασιλικός means "royal/of the king" (likely an official of Herod Antipas). "Official" is sufficient; "government" and "royal" are both valid qualifiers.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor divergence.

---

## 4:48 — "Unless you see signs and wonders you will not believe" — framing

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Unless you see signs and wonders, you will not believe." | Literal; statement |
| LOGOS | "Unless you see signs and wonders you will not believe." | Formal; same |
| PNEUMA | "Will you never believe unless you see signs and wonders?" | Dynamic; converts to question form |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. The Greek is a conditional statement (ἐὰν μὴ...οὐ μὴ πιστεύσητε), not a question. PNEUMA converts it to a question, changing the rhetorical force.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:49 — "Sir, come down before my child dies" vs. "Lord, please come before my little boy dies"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Lord, come down before the child of me dies." | "Lord"; "child" (παιδίον) |
| LOGOS | "Sir, come down before my child dies." | "Sir" |
| PNEUMA | "Lord, please come before my little boy dies." | "Lord"; adds "please" and "little boy" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. "Sir" is appropriate for κύριε in this context (a petitioner addressing a healer). PNEUMA's "little boy" and "please" add emotional color not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:51 — "his son was recovering" vs. "his son was alive and well" for Greek ὁ παῖς αὐτοῦ ζῇ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the boy of him lives" | Literal; present tense "lives" |
| LOGOS | "his son was recovering" | Formal; interprets ζῇ in context |
| PNEUMA | "his son was alive and well" | Dynamic; adds "and well" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. The Greek ζῇ simply means "lives/is living." LOGOS's "recovering" captures the narrative context (the servants report improvement). PNEUMA's "and well" adds beyond the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:52 — "the seventh hour" vs. "one o'clock" for Greek ὥραν ἑβδόμην

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "[at the] seventh hour" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "at the seventh hour" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "at one o'clock" | Dynamic; converts to modern time |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "the seventh hour." Consistent with the approach to other time references. PNEUMA's conversion assumes counting from 6 AM.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 4:54 — "second sign" vs. "second miraculous sign"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "second sign the Yeshua did" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "the second sign that Jesus did" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "the second miraculous sign Jesus did" | Dynamic; adds "miraculous" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. John consistently uses σημεῖον ("sign"), not "miraculous sign." Adding "miraculous" undermines John's deliberate vocabulary choice.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

# Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive expansion (adding words) | 12 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| PNEUMA dynamic paraphrase (restructuring) | 6 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| EMET literal structure / transliteration | 8 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| Time reference conversion | 2 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS (ancient reference kept) |
| κύριε as "Sir" vs. "Lord" | 2 | Context-dependent; generally "Sir" for strangers |
| Textual variant (ὁ κύριος vs. Ἰησοῦς) | 1 | Resolved in favor of Sinaiticus reading |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

Chapter 4 introduces the most sustained dialogue in the Gospels, and PNEUMA's dynamic approach becomes most visible here. PNEUMA adds interpretive words ("please," "dear," "little boy," "only"), converts ancient time references, adds explanatory sentences not in the text (4:26), and intensifies emotional register ("shocked," "had the nerve"). The most notable textual divergence is in 4:1, where PNEUMA substitutes "Jesus" for the Sinaiticus reading "the Lord." EMET continues to transliterate all names and preserve Greek syntax. LOGOS consistently provides the middle-ground rendering that SOPHIA adopts as consensus.

The chapter also highlights the recurring κύριε translation question: SOPHIA follows LOGOS in rendering it contextually as "Sir" (when addressed by strangers) rather than EMET's consistent "Lord."

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
